source_claim: Kaṇṇaki identifies herself to the hill women as the one who lost her husband when Madurai and its kingship were destroyed
The hill community is instructed to receive the one-breasted woman as a deity within its own settlement
Drums horn bell song incense flower offerings and protective boundaries form the first local worship system
Waterfalls pools stone mineral colour flowers and mountain water structure the women’s ecological world
The kuravai links several Murukaṉ landscapes through the spear and remembered divine combat
Possession ritual and Murukaṉ worship are debated as responses to love-sickness and desired marriage
The women convert the destruction of unjust Madurai into a communal song praising the Pattini
Senguttuvan and Ilango Venmal leave the Vañci court complex to view the mountain country with their retinue
Kuravai songs field calls drums waterfalls elephant keepers hunters and the moving army create a layered hill soundscape
Hill people present ivory eaglewood honey sandal pigment spices fruit sugarcane and cultivated produce as tribute

zone_or_system: VAN-Z03 animal tribute and forest knowledge
source_claim: Young wild animals birds and forest species are carried to court as signs of mountain access and specialised ecological knowledge
The Kuravar report seeing the solitary one-breasted woman beneath the vēṅkai tree ascend with her husband
Sāttanār narrates to the Cēra court the anklet case the burning of Madurai and the deaths of the Pāṇḍya king and queen
Senguttuvan reflects that rulers fear failed rain unjust death oppressive rule and the burden of protecting subjects
Ilango Venmal recommends honouring the Pattini who reached their country rather than treating the episode only as foreign tragedy
